narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, LSAT score of 25, Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the 1st decile, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted an 88.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your undergraduate GPA, each pulling the prediction in specific directions compared to typical students. Your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the average of around the 5th or 6th decile—had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school lagged significantly behind most peers who pass the bar. Your LSAT score of 25, well under the typical 37 seen in the dataset, was the second biggest driver pushing the prediction toward failure, as lower scores like yours are strongly linked to bar struggles in the model's view of past students. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50—higher than the average 3.21—worked against the failure prediction, helping to offset some of the risk and showing your college strengths compared to many applicants. While your middle income and full-time status are common and didn't sway things much, these top academic markers dominated the model's thinking for someone with your background. It's tough to hear, especially as an Asian male where high achievement is often expected, but this rejection highlights areas like your deciles and LSAT that the model sees as mismatched with bar success patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 37 average, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, or exploring programs with more flexible admissions that weigh your solid UGPA more heavily. Building bar prep experience through targeted courses could also help demonstrate your potential beyond these metrics. With focused improvements, you can reshape your profile for stronger future applications.
